skill.name: dual-disease-transcriptomic-ml-planner
skill.description: Generates complete dual-disease transcriptomic + machine learning research designs from a user-provided disease pair. Use when users want to identify shared DEGs, common hub genes, cross-disease biomarkers, or shared molecular mechanisms between two diseases using public GEO data. Triggers:"shared biomarker study for two diseases", "dual-disease transcriptomic ML paper", "identify common DEGs between disease A and B", "cross-disease hub gene discovery", "shared DEG + PPI + ROC design", "immune infiltration shared biomarker", or "I want to study disease X and Y together". Always outputs four workload configurations (Lite / Standard / Advanced / Publication+) with a recommended primary plan, step-by-step workflow, figure plan, validation strategy, minimal executable version, and publication upgrade path.
skill.summary: Generates complete dual-disease transcriptomic + machine learning research designs from a user-provided disease pair
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 75 static findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (40+ instances) refer to bioinformatics method names (MCC = Maximum Clique Centrality, DESeq2, CIBERSORT, EPIC) not cryptographic algorithms. The 'backtick execution' findings reference R package installation commands in documentation examples. The 'hardcoded URL' and 'reconnaissance' findings reference legitimate public bioinformatics databases (NCBI GEO, CIBERSORT, TIMER2.0). The heuristic 'dangerous combination' finding is a composite of already-dismissed patterns. This is a documentation/reference skill with no executable code, no credential access, and no data exfiltration capability. All R code examples use clearly labeled placeholder GEO IDs.

content.user_title: Plan dual-disease transcriptomic studies with ML
content.value_statement: Researchers need a structured way to design comparative transcriptomic studies between two diseases using public data. This skill generates complete research plans with four workload levels, figure templates, and publication upgrade paths.
actual_capabilities: Generates four-tier research plans (Lite, Standard, Advanced, Publication+) from a disease pair
Recommends GEO datasets and bioinformatics tools by disease class
Creates step-by-step workflows for DEG analysis, PPI networks, and hub gene prioritization
Provides immune infiltration analysis guidance with tissue-appropriate deconvolution tools
Outputs figure plans, validation strategies, and publication upgrade paths
Includes validated R code templates with fault tolerance guards for common failure modes
limitations: Cannot execute code or run bioinformatics analyses directly
Requires public GEO data availability for both diseases to generate useful plans
Does not provide experimental validation or wet-lab protocols
Immune deconvolution accuracy depends on tissue type and tool selection

use_cases.title: Cross-disease biomarker discovery for researchers
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher studying shared molecular mechanisms between two conditions generates a complete study design with pipeline recommendations.

use_cases.title: Publication planning for graduate students
use_cases.description: A graduate student designing a dual-disease transcriptomic study receives a structured workflow with a clear publication pathway.

use_cases.title: Grant proposal methodology design
use_cases.description: A principal investigator outlines a strong bioinformatics approach for a grant application with detailed methodology and validation strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Basic dual-disease study plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Identify shared biomarkers between type 2 diabetes and NAFLD using public GEO data. I need a complete study plan with all four workload options.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user with limited bioinformatics experience studying two metabolic diseases

prompt_templates.title: Time-constrained study design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a dual-disease transcriptomic ML study for Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. I have 8 weeks and two GEO datasets per disease. Include immune infiltration analysis.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher with specific time and data constraints

prompt_templates.title: Complete pipeline with R code framework
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a shared DEG, PPI, and ROC study for colorectal cancer and C. difficile infection. Include full R code with validation cohort and immune analysis.
prompt_templates.scenario: Experienced bioinformatician needing a structured pipeline template

prompt_templates.title: High-impact publication design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Publication+ level study for intracranial aneurysm and abdominal aortic aneurysm. Include multi-cohort validation, cross-platform reproducibility, and drug target analysis.
prompt_templates.scenario: Principal investigator targeting a high-impact journal

output_examples.input: I want to study shared hub genes between intracranial aneurysm and abdominal aortic aneurysm using public GEO data.
output_examples.output: Generated study includes four workload configurations: Lite (2-4 weeks), Standard (5-9 weeks), Advanced (9-14 weeks), and Publication+ (12-20 weeks). Standard is recommended due to ECM biology relevance and multiple available GEO datasets. Workflow covers DEG analysis, PPI construction with CytoHubba consensus (5 algorithms), ROC validation, and arterial wall immune infiltration.

output_examples.input: Plan a dual-disease study for SLE and RA with only one GEO dataset per disease.
output_examples.output: Lite configuration recommended due to single-dataset constraint. Validation ROC declared infeasible with clear limitation statement. GEO search strategy provided for sourcing second datasets. Blood and PBMC recommended as compatible tissue for both autoimmune diseases.

best_practices: Always specify both disease names and any time or data constraints for the most relevant configuration recommendation.
Review the references folder files before starting for disease-specific GEO search strategies and tool selection guidance.
Use EXAMPLE ID convention in R code to avoid accidentally running pipelines with placeholder accession numbers.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for single-disease studies or wet-lab experimental design it will redirect to appropriate alternatives.
Do not skip the tissue matching step when diseases require different tissue types this can invalidate cross-disease comparisons.
Do not interpret hub gene candidates as mechanistic proof they are biomarker candidates requiring experimental validation.

faq.question: What data sources does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill uses public GEO (Gene Expression Omnibus) transcriptomic data. It provides search strategies and example accession numbers for various disease classes.

faq.question: Can I get executable code from this skill?
faq.answer: The skill provides R code templates with guard clauses and EXAMPLE ID placeholders. You must replace these with real GEO accession numbers before running.

faq.question: What is the difference between the four workload levels?
faq.answer: Lite is a pilot study with one dataset per disease. Standard adds validation ROC. Advanced adds immune and GSEA analysis. Publication+ adds multi-cohort and cross-platform validation.

faq.question: What happens if the diseases share no common genes?
faq.answer: The skill includes a recovery sequence: relax the logFC threshold, use the top 500 DEGs, or switch to WGCNA co-expression module overlap.

faq.question: Can I use this for non-transcriptomic data like proteomics?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed specifically for GEO transcriptomic data. Non-transcriptomic requests trigger the input validation redirect.

faq.question: Does this skill perform actual bioinformatics analysis?
faq.answer: No. This is a research planning skill that generates study designs, workflow steps, and code templates. Data analysis must be performed separately.
